title: The Star Tarot Card Meaning & Best Crystals
name: The Star
theme: Hope, renewal, guidance after the storm
upright_meaning: A figure kneels at the edge of water, pouring water from two vessels — one into the pool, one onto the land — a great eight-pointed star above surrounded by smaller stars, a bird in a tree nearby. The Star is the archetype of hope after the storm — the calm that follows The Tower, the guidance that returns. Upright, it speaks to renewal, to reconnecting with what gives you meaning, and to the quiet faith that the way forward is being shown.
reversed_meaning: Reversed, The Star often points to a temporary disconnection from hope — feeling adrift, faith worn thin by difficulty, the guidance obscured by discouragement. It is not a verdict of despair; it is an invitation to ask what has dimmed your sense of meaning, and to let even a small light back in.
psychological_lens: hope theory (the capacity to identify pathways toward what you want and the agency to begin moving — hope as a skill, not just a feeling)

eastern_anchors.tibetan: The Star resonates with the Eastern ideal of '菩提心' (the awakened heart/mind) — the steady, luminous aspiration that re-arises even after difficulty, not as denial but as renewed direction.
eastern_anchors.crystal_culture: Aquamarine, the stone of calm courage and clear water, embodies The Star's serene renewal — the still pool this card depicts.

recommended_practice: name one thing that has renewed even a little hope recently, however small, and one next step toward it

best_overall.name: Aquamarine
best_overall.reason: Aquamarine is the stone of calm courage and clear renewal — The Star's still pool in mineral form. Hold it when you need to reconnect with hope after a hard season; its cool, clear energy supports the serene faith this card offers, the quiet trust that the way is being shown.

best_upright.name: Selenite
best_upright.reason: Selenite is the stone of clarity and cleansing light. For an upright Star draw, it supports the cleared, lifted state of mind in which guidance can be perceived again — the bright sky after the storm.

best_reversed.name: Lepidolite
best_reversed.reason: When The Star reverses into despair or thin faith, Lepidolite (naturally containing calming lithium) gently soothes the discouraged nervous system. It is the stone for the tender work of letting hope back in, without forcing it.

best_love.name: Rose Quartz
best_love.reason: Rose Quartz brings warmth to The Star's love readings, where a relationship may be renewing after difficulty. It supports reopening the heart in trust, gently, after it has been through a storm.

best_daily_wear.name: Moonstone
best_daily_wear.reason: Moonstone is a daily stone of new beginnings and inner light. Worn as jewelry, it keeps The Star's hope within reach — a soft reminder that guidance returns, even on ordinary cloudy days.

excerpt: The Star is the card of hope renewed after the storm. Explore its upright and reversed meanings, the best crystals for calm renewal, and a short practice for tending the small lights that guide the way forward.

content:
When The Star appears in a reading, it often arrives like calm after a storm — the card of hope renewed, of guidance returning, of the quiet faith that the way forward is being shown. Card 17 of the Major Arcana, it is the archetype of the hopeful. Paired with the right crystals, this card becomes an invitation to reconnect with what gives you meaning, to tend even a small light, and to trust the renewal that follows even the hardest passages.

The Star at a Glance

The Star is the card of hope, renewal, faith, and guidance after difficulty. Upright, it points to a season of healing and renewed direction; reversed, it often asks where hope has been dimmed by discouragement. The crystal most often paired with The Star is Aquamarine, the stone of calm courage and clear renewal.

  • Archetype: The Hopeful
  • Core theme: Hope, renewal, guidance after the storm
  • Recommended crystal: Aquamarine
  • Upright: hope, faith, renewal
  • Reversed: despair, disconnection from hope, faith lost

The Star Upright: Meaning & Message

In the Rider-Waite image, a figure kneels at the edge of a pool of water, pouring water from two vessels — one into the pool, one onto the land in a small stream. A great eight-pointed star shines above, surrounded by seven smaller stars. A bird perches in a tree on the shore. The figure is naked, unguarded, and the scene is wholly calm — the stillness that follows a storm (The Tower, card 16, comes just before). The water is the renewal; the stars are the guidance; the nakedness is the vulnerability that hope requires. Upright, The Star is the archetype of hope after the storm. It is the card of the moment the worst has passed and a quieter, clearer light returns. In everyday terms, The Star often appears after a difficult period — when the dust of The Tower has settled, when grief or struggle has begun to ease — and points toward renewal, reconnection with meaning, and the slow return of faith in the path forward. This is not naïve optimism. The Star knows the storm happened. Its hope is the kind that has been through difficulty and chosen, deliberately, to stay open anyway — to tend a small light rather than to conclude that the dark is permanent.

The Star Reversed: The Shadow Aspect

When The Star appears reversed, the same hope has been dimmed somewhere. The familiar form is disconnection from meaning — faith worn thin by sustained difficulty, a sense of being adrift, the guidance feeling obscured by discouragement or exhaustion. It is not the absence of hope so much as the temporary loss of contact with it. This is not a verdict of despair. A reversed card is information for reflection. The Star reversed often asks one honest question: what has dimmed your sense of meaning lately — and what is one small light you could let back in, rather than waiting for the whole sky to clear?

A reflection prompt: Name one thing that has renewed even a flicker of hope recently, however small — a kindness, a moment of beauty, a small sign of progress. Let yourself stay with it for a minute, rather than dismissing it. The Star returns through such small lights.

Best Crystals for The Star

Crystals paired with The Star tend to share qualities of calm, renewal, or soothing clarity, matching the card's theme of hope after the storm. The five below are drawn from the major crystal-and-tarot references and mineral tradition, chosen for distinct aspects of this card — from the calm-renewal stone itself, to a stone of cleansing light, to a soothing stone for when faith has worn thin.

Best Overall Crystal for The Star

Aquamarine is the stone of calm courage and clear renewal — The Star's still pool in mineral form. Hold it when you need to reconnect with hope after a hard season; its cool, clear energy supports the serene faith this card offers, the quiet trust that the way is being shown even when you cannot see the whole path.

Aquamarine — Aquamarine is the stone of calm courage and clear renewal — The Star's still pool in mineral form. Hold it when you need to reconnect with hope after a hard season; its cool, clear energy supports the serene faith this card offers, the quiet trust that the way is being shown. Read Aquamarine Meaning → · Shop Aquamarine →

Best Crystal for Upright The Star Energy

Selenite is the stone of clarity and cleansing light. For an upright Star draw, when the storm has passed and the sky is clearing, it supports the lifted, cleansed state of mind in which guidance can be perceived again — the bright sky after the darkness, made tangible.

Selenite — Selenite is the stone of clarity and cleansing light. For an upright Star draw, it supports the cleared, lifted state of mind in which guidance can be perceived again — the bright sky after the storm. Explore Selenite in depth → · Browse Selenite jewelry →

Best Crystal for Reversed The Star (Shadow Work)

When The Star reverses into despair or thin faith, Lepidolite (naturally containing calming lithium) gently soothes the discouraged nervous system. It is the stone for the tender work of letting hope back in, without forcing it — the steady, gentle presence that hope requires when it has been bruised.

Lepidolite — When The Star reverses into despair or thin faith, Lepidolite (naturally containing calming lithium) gently soothes the discouraged nervous system. It is the stone for the tender work of letting hope back in, without forcing it. Find your Lepidolite piece →

Best Crystal for Love & Relationship Readings

Rose Quartz brings warmth to The Star's love readings, where a relationship may be renewing after difficulty. It supports reopening the heart in trust, gently, after it has been through a storm — the willingness to hope again in connection, without denying what came before.

Rose Quartz — Rose Quartz brings warmth to The Star's love readings, where a relationship may be renewing after difficulty. It supports reopening the heart in trust, gently, after it has been through a storm. Read Rose Quartz Meaning → · Shop Rose Quartz →

Best Crystal to Wear Daily

Moonstone is a daily stone of new beginnings and inner light. Worn as jewelry, it keeps The Star's hope within reach — a soft reminder that guidance returns, even on ordinary cloudy days when the sky feels far away.

Moonstone — Moonstone is a daily stone of new beginnings and inner light. Worn as jewelry, it keeps The Star's hope within reach — a soft reminder that guidance returns, even on ordinary cloudy days. Browse Moonstone jewelry →

Using The Star Crystals in Your Tarot Practice

A short practice to bring The Star's energy into your reading or your day. Keep it practical — crystals are a focus for intention, not magic.

You'll need: Aquamarine or Selenite.

Step 1 — Set the focus (1 min): Name one thing that has renewed even a small flicker of hope recently — a kindness received, a moment of unexpected beauty, a tiny sign of progress. Stay with it for a full minute, letting it be real, rather than dismissing it as too small to matter.

Step 2 — Hold the stone + draw a card (2 min): Hold your Aquamarine over your heart and draw a card with the question "what is the next small step the light is showing me?" Let the stone's calm support the receptive state in which guidance can be felt, as you sit with whatever the card surfaces.

Step 3 — Close with one commitment: Close by naming one small act of renewal you will offer yourself this week — rest, beauty, reconnection with something that matters — and commit to it. Place the stone somewhere visible until you take it. The Star's hope is not被动等待; it is tended, one small light at a time.

Prefer something quicker? Touch your Moonstone jewelry each morning and ask: "What small light can I notice or tend today, even if the larger sky still feels clouded?" The card's gift is renewed hope; this small ritual trains the habit of attending to the lights that are present rather than only the dark.

Crystals to Support The Star Reversed

When The Star appears reversed, it often points to despair, disconnection from hope, faith lost — the card's energy in shadow rather than a bad omen. It's an invitation to notice where hope and faith has gone into overdrive or gone quiet. The crystal below is traditionally used to support this specific reflection.

Supporting stone — Lepidolite: Lepidolite is the steady stone for the shadow work this reversal invites. When hope has been dimmed by sustained difficulty, hold a piece against your heart and let its naturally calming presence soothe the discouraged nervous system — without forcing optimism on a system that needs gentleness first. Pair it with the Aquamarine above once the settling has done its work; together they restore the calm in which hope can return on its own.

The Star Through Three Lenses

1. Tarot tradition: In the Rider-Waite tradition, The Star is numbered 17 and corresponds to Aquarius. It is read as the archetype of hope, renewal, and guidance after the storm — the calm that follows The Tower's upheaval. The eight-pointed star is sometimes associated with Sirius or with the star of the magi; the water poured into pool and land marks the renewal of both inner and outer life. The card is widely considered one of the most gently hopeful in the deck.

2. Psychological archetype: In psychological terms, The Star maps closely onto the well-researched psychology of hope — most fully developed in the work of C. R. Snyder, who defined hope not as a feeling but as a skill: the capacity to identify pathways toward what you want and the agency to begin moving along them. Research consistently links higher hope to resilience, well-being, and the ability to navigate difficulty without collapsing into despair. Read through this lens, The Star is not promising that everything will be fine. It is reminding you that hope is something you do — a practice of noticing the next small step and taking it — and that the practice is available even after hard things.

This lens treats The Star as a mirror for self-inquiry — the psychological dimension here is hope theory & optimism — rather than a prediction.

3. Crystal companion: As a crystal companion, Aquamarine becomes the tangible anchor for the calm, clear renewal this card asks. The Star reminds you to reconnect with hope; the stone gives you a cool, steady focus to hold while you do. Worn as jewelry, it is a small daily cue to stay receptive to the lights that are present — the kind of calm, open attention that lets guidance be noticed, rather than the braced defensiveness that the storm (understandably) left behind.

The Star in the Eastern Tradition

The Star resonates with the Eastern ideal of 菩提心 (pútíxīn) — the awakened heart/mind, the steady, luminous aspiration that re-arises even after difficulty. In the Mahayana Buddhist tradition, this quality is not naive optimism; it is the deliberate cultivation of a compassionate, directed aspiration that survives contact with suffering precisely because it is not dependent on circumstances being easy. For The Star, this echo reframes the card's hope as a renewable inner resource — something that can be re-kindled deliberately, rather than a fleeting mood that circumstance simply grants or withholds.

Aquamarine, the stone of calm water and clear courage, embodies The Star's serene renewal in a form that crosses cultures. The stone has been associated across traditions with the soothing, clarifying quality of still water — exactly the pool the figure kneels beside in the Rider-Waite image. For The Star, the cross-cultural reverence for aquamarine as the stone of calm and clear courage pairs naturally with the Western crystal-and-tarot reading of it as the anchor of renewed hope.

A Mini Crystal Tarot Spread for The Star

A three-card spread to explore The Star's message in your life, with a crystal paired to each position.

PositionQuestionCompanion Crystal
1. Where The Star is showing upWhere is The Hopeful energy active in my life right now?Aquamarine
2. What it invitesWhat is this card asking me to notice or do?Selenite
3. The next stepWhat small action aligns me with this card's healthy expression?Moonstone

Shuffle your deck, place a stone on each position as you draw, and sit with the three cards together for a few minutes before journaling.

Tarot and crystal meanings are based on spiritual traditions, symbolism, and personal mindfulness practices. They are a tool for self-reflection and contemplation, not a substitute for medical, financial, or professional advice — and not a prediction of fixed outcomes.

Frequently Asked Questions

What does The Star mean in tarot?The Star (card 17 of the Major Arcana) is the card of hope, renewal, faith, and guidance after difficulty. It corresponds to Aquarius and is traditionally read as the archetype of the calm that follows the storm — the quiet return of light and direction after upheaval. In a reading, it often points to a season of healing, reconnection with meaning, and renewed trust in the path forward.
What is the upright meaning of The Star?Upright, The Star points to hope renewed and a season of healing. It suggests that the worst of a difficult passage has passed, that guidance is returning, and that the work now is to reconnect with what gives you meaning and to trust the quiet faith that the way is being shown. It is often read as one of the most gently hopeful cards in the deck.
What are the best crystals for The Star?The crystals most often paired with The Star are Aquamarine (for calm courage and clear renewal), Selenite (for cleansing light), Lepidolite (to soothe discouragement when hope is dim), Rose Quartz (to reopen the heart after a storm), and Moonstone (for daily renewed inner light). The principle across references is calm, hopeful, renewing energy.
What does The Star mean in love?In love readings, The Star often points to a relationship renewing after difficulty — trust slowly returning, connection re-deepening after a hard passage. It can also signal the possibility of new, hopeful connection after a period of withdrawal. The invitation is to let the heart reopen gently, without denying what came before but without letting it close the door on what could be.
What does The Star reversed mean?Reversed, The Star often points to a temporary disconnection from hope — faith worn thin by sustained difficulty, a sense of being adrift. It is not a verdict of despair — it is an invitation to ask what has dimmed your sense of meaning, and to let even one small light back in, tending it patiently rather than waiting for the whole sky to clear at once.
What chakra is The Star associated with?The crystals paired with The Star work primarily with the Heart and Third Eye chakras — Aquamarine, Rose Quartz, and Moonstone for the calm, open heart, and Selenite for the lifted, clear perspective of the Third Eye. The card's energy lives where open-heartedness meets clear seeing.
Is The Star a bad card?In tarot, no card is inherently 'good' or 'bad' — every card, including reversed positions, is read as information for reflection rather than a fixed outcome. For The Star, the card's "hope and renewal" can read as discouragement or lost faith when reversed — but the invitation is simply to tend one small light rather than to demand the whole sky, not to treat the reversed card as a forecast of permanent darkness.
Can tarot predict the future?No — tarot is a symbolic system used for self-reflection and contemplation, not a tool that predicts fixed outcomes. The cards surface patterns and questions; your choices shape what happens next. For The Star, the card may point to the renewal that follows a hard passage — but whether you tend the small lights that return, and how you act on the guidance they offer, remains entirely in your hands.

Related Tarot Cards

  • The Tower: The Awakener — Sudden revelation, structural collapse, the truth that breaks what was unstable
  • The Sun: The Radiant — Joy, vitality, clarity, success
  • All Tarot Card Crystals → (Hub)

The Star is the card of the calm that follows the storm, and of the quiet faith that returns when the worst has passed. Pair it with a crystal that supports renewed hope, and let it be a reminder that hope is not a feeling that arrives but a practice you tend — one small light at a time — and that the willingness to stay open after difficulty is one of the most renewable resources a life can have.
